topic: A neighbor asks to use part of the yard for a garden
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun beat down, turning the already patchy lawn into a shimmering, oppressive expanse. I was wrestling with a stubborn patch of weeds near the fence line when the sound of shuffling footsteps approached. It was Mr. Henderson, my neighbor, and already, I could feel the familiar tightening in my chest. "Excuse me, Sarah," he began, his voice dripping with an unwarranted cheerfulness. "I was wondering if I might borrow a small section of your yard? Just a little bit for a vegetable patch." I stopped pulling at the tenacious roots, letting the trowel clatter onto the grass. I slowly straightened up, wiping a bead of sweat from my temple with the back of my wrist. My jaw tightened, and I stared pointedly at the boundary marker. "Mr. Henderson," I managed, my tone carefully level, "that area is designated for my perennial border. I'm afraid I can't accommodate that request."

[story 2]

I was midway through pruning my prize-winning roses, the scent of damp earth and blooming petals usually a soothing balm, but today it just felt suffocating. Then, there he was, leaning against my mailbox, hands clasped behind his back. "Hey, Clara! Listen, I have this idea for a little herb garden," he started, his eyes wide and hopeful. "Could I possibly use that strip by the old oak tree? It would be perfect for rosemary." I paused, the shears hovering mid-air. I let out a slow, deliberate breath, the kind that signals deep internal strain. I turned fully toward him, my posture stiffening. "Mr. Henderson," I said, my voice dangerously quiet, "that space is strictly private. I've already planned out every inch of that border." I crossed my arms tightly over my chest, refusing to offer any further explanation.

[story 3]

The sheer audacity of the request hit me like a physical blow. I was trying to finish setting up the new bird feeder, a simple task that usually brought a quiet sense of accomplishment, but now every movement felt labored. When the doorbell rang, it was him. He stood on my porch, beaming, clutching a crumpled piece of paper. "Hi there! I was hoping you could help me out," he chirped. "I need a little bit of space in your yard—just a small corner for some tomatoes. Would you mind terribly?" I lowered the screwdriver, my knuckles white where I gripped the handle. I looked him dead in the eye, my expression hardening into something unyielding. "No, Mr. Henderson," I stated flatly, my voice devoid of warmth. "My yard is not available for borrowing. That's a firm no."
